title: Using Document Similarity Methods to create Parallel Datasets for Code Translation

structured.summary: The paper proposes using document similarity measures, particularly Word Mover’s Distance, to automatically mine noisy parallel source code datasets from non-parallel corpora, facilitating supervised code translation across multiple programming languages. It demonstrates that translation models trained on these noisy datasets maintain robust performance, and extends experiments to a broader set of languages beyond prior work. The approach offers a practical solution to the scarcity of parallel code data, validated through extensive empirical evaluation.

strengths.point: Effective use of document similarity methods for mining noisy parallel code datasets
strengths.evidence: WMD-based similarity consistently outperforms classical metrics (TF-IDF, BM25, LSI, LDA) in match and pseudo-match accuracy across multiple datasets including CodeNet.

strengths.point: Demonstrated robustness of code translation models to noise in training data
strengths.evidence: Models trained on datasets with up to ~30% noise show only modest degradation (1-2% drop) in BLEU, CodeBLEU, and exact match scores.

strengths.point: Extension to multiple and diverse programming languages
strengths.evidence: Mining and evaluation performed on 10 languages from CodeNet, showing improved translation accuracy over random pairing and enabling supervised training beyond common language pairs.

weaknesses.point: Lack of comparison with recent neural code similarity embeddings
weaknesses.evidence: No ablation or benchmark against pretrained neural models like CodeBERT or GraphCodeBERT embeddings for similarity search, which may yield better alignment quality.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient justification and exploration of similarity threshold choice and alignment constraints
weaknesses.evidence: Threshold δ selection appears heuristic without sensitivity analysis; one-to-one matching assumption excludes many-to-one or partial code alignments.

weaknesses.point: Evaluation metrics do not fully assess semantic correctness or functional equivalence
weaknesses.evidence: Reliance on BLEU, CodeBLEU, and exact match without runtime validation or unit test-based correctness for most language pairs limits insight into translation quality.

weaknesses.point: Limited reproducibility details and no public code or dataset release stated
weaknesses.evidence: Missing precise preprocessing, tokenization, hyperparameters, and no explicit statement on dataset or code availability.

weaknesses.point: No statistical significance testing or confidence intervals reported
weaknesses.evidence: Performance improvements (~1-2%) lack tests to confirm robustness beyond variance.

questions.question: How would similarity-based mining perform using neural code embeddings tailored for semantic similarity?
questions.motivation: Assessing this would clarify whether WMD truly outperforms or if neural approaches can further improve dataset quality.

questions.question: What is the impact of relaxing the one-to-one matching constraint to allow many-to-one or partial code alignments?
questions.motivation: This could increase dataset size and quality by capturing realistic code correspondence patterns.

questions.question: How do noisy parallel datasets affect semantic correctness and potential downstream bugs in translated code?
questions.motivation: Understanding error types and mitigation strategies is crucial for safe real-world deployment.

questions.question: Can fine-grained alignment at function or code block level improve mining and translation performance compared to document-level alignment?
questions.motivation: Finer granularity may yield higher quality pairs and better translation fidelity.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of comparison with recent neural code similarity embeddings. No ablation or benchmark against pretrained neural models like CodeBERT or GraphCodeBERT embeddings for similarity search, which may yield better alignment quality
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient justification and exploration of similarity threshold choice and alignment constraints. Threshold δ selection appears heuristic without sensitivity analysis; one-to-one matching assumption excludes many-to-one or partial code alignments
critique_points.point_003: Evaluation metrics do not fully assess semantic correctness or functional equivalence. Reliance on BLEU, CodeBLEU, and exact match without runtime validation or unit test-based correctness for most language pairs limits insight into translation quality
critique_points.point_004: Limited reproducibility details and no public code or dataset release stated. Missing precise preprocessing, tokenization, hyperparameters, and no explicit statement on dataset or code availability
critique_points.point_005: No statistical significance testing or confidence intervals reported. Performance improvements (~1-2%) lack tests to confirm robustness beyond variance
